title: Cat Facial Twitching: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's face twitching rhythmically? Learn about focal seizures, muscle fasciculations, and when to seek veterinary care for your feline friend.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rhythmic facial twitching in cats is often a sign of focal seizures or muscle fasciculations and requires a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours, though prolonged episodes or full-body convulsions require immediate emergency care. Capturing a video of the event is the most helpful tool for your veterinarian to reach a diagnosis.

What does it mean if my cat has rhythmic facial twitching?

Rhythmic muscle twitching in a cat's face, often involving the eyelids, whiskers, or mouth, is frequently identified as a focal seizure or muscle fasciculation. Unlike generalized seizures, focal seizures affect a specific area of the brain, causing localized movements while the cat may remain conscious but appear dazed or unresponsive.

Is my cat’s facial twitching considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for rhythmic facial twitching is categorized as Medium.
  • While a single, brief episode may not be an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a clinical sign of neurological, metabolic, or toxicological dysfunction that requires a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the twitching lasts more than five minutes, occurs multiple times in one day, or progresses to full-body convulsions.

How can taking a video or photo help the veterinarian triage my cat?

  • Because these episodes are often intermittent, your cat may act completely normal by the time they reach the clinic.
  • Capturing a clear video of the twitching is one of the most valuable tools you can provide to help the vet observe the frequency, duration, and specific muscle groups involved.
  • Documentation is essential for differentiating between neurological seizures, dental pain, or behavioral disorders.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, facial twitching or paralysis can stem from idiopathic facial paralysis, trigeminal neuritis, or trauma. Idiopathic facial paralysis results in unilateral or bilateral paresis or paralysis of facial muscles, with breeds like domestic longhaired cats at increased risk; clinical signs include an inability to blink, drooping ear, drooping upper lip, and drooling. Trigeminal neuritis, though uncommon in cats, leads to acute onset of flaccid jaw paralysis, difficulty eating and drinking, and possibly Horner's syndrome or decreased facial sensation; spontaneous recovery usually occurs within 3-4 weeks. Trauma from rough handling, automobile accidents, or surgery can also cause facial nerve injuries.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1239)

faqs.question: Could dental issues cause facial twitching?
faqs.answer: Yes, severe dental disease, resorptive lesions, or oral infections can cause 'chattering' or rhythmic jaw and cheek movements that resemble twitching.

faqs.question: What should I do during an active twitching episode?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat safe by moving them away from stairs or sharp edges. Do not put your hands near their mouth, and record a video of the event to show your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Can toxins cause facial twitching in cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, exposure to certain household toxins, such as lilies or permethrin (often found in canine flea treatments), can cause neurological symptoms including facial tremors.
